metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete Pydantic field names, types, and allowed values for ALL new fields in `code_review_verification_model()` — tier-tagged correctness binaries, tier-tagged maintainability binaries, `silent_failure` (bool/str), and `escapes_automation` (bool/str) — co-located in the plan's schema section.
findings.finding: The plan names `code_review_verification_model()` as a new schema extending the shared model, but neither the new schema class nor its new fields (tier-tagged binaries, silent_failure, escapes_automation) are defined anywhere in the plan or codebase.
findings.location: ## Implementation mechanics + calibration data — 'SUB-ANSWER SCHEMA: emit the tier-tagged binaries as new fields inside the SINGLE aggregate Pass-2 verify structured output (a `code_review_verification_model()` extending the shared model)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether the new tier-tagged carved sub-questions participate in `validity()` for code-review findings: either (a) they do not (validity remains the shared GRADED_BINARY set, impact_code alone uses them), or (b) a code-review-specific validity function is introduced alongside `impact_code` — and confirm plan-review validity is unaffected either way.
findings.finding: The plan does not state whether the new tier-tagged carved sub-questions (serious={data-loss-without-recovery, ...}, moderate/minor={capability-degraded, ...}) extend, replace, or run alongside the existing GRADED_BINARY set for the `validity()` computation. If they are new schema fields in `code_review_verification_model` but NOT in GRADED_BINARY, validity will score the old 7-question set while impact_code scores the new set, creating a disconnect: a finding can have `data-loss-without-recovery=yes` (high impact_code) but be dropped by validity if the old questions (path_reachable, evidence_entails_finding, etc.) score low — which may be appropriate, but this structural relationship is undeclared.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics — 'SUB-ANSWER SCHEMA'; What section — 'CARVED SUB-QUESTION SET'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a live/end-to-end acceptance criterion that routes at least one realistic code-review finding through the full pipeline (`verify → code_review_verification_model() → decide.py with impact_fn=impact_code`) and asserts the resulting impact score matches the expected tier, confirming the dispatch wiring and schema integration are correct end-to-end.
findings.finding: The newly-defaulted `impact_code` path (code-review gates dispatching via `impact_fn=impact_code`) is validated only through offline labeled-fixture simulation, with no live/end-to-end acceptance criterion that exercises the actual dispatch wiring through `decide.py → pass3_decide(impact_fn=impact_code)`. The suite can go green while the dispatch is broken.
findings.location: AC3 ('Per-gate dispatch via `impact_fn`') and AC4 ('simulation on it shows the model separates HIGH from NIT')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'An integration test (or dogfood smoke test) exercises a labeled HIGH finding end-to-end through `pass3_decide(impact_fn=impact_code)` and asserts the returned impact ≥ block threshold, AND a labeled NIT finding returns impact ≤ 0.25; this test must invoke the real `impact_code` function through the dispatch, not a direct formula call.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether prod_lane likelihood (`common/sometimes/rare`) is a new schema field (and give its name) or a replacement of the existing `likelihood` enum in `SeverityAttrs`, and update the processing-rule map accordingly in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `likelihood` enum vocabulary (`common=1.0/sometimes=0.6/rare=0.25`) for prod_lane that conflicts with the existing `SeverityAttrs.likelihood` field (`low|medium|high`) in the live `verification_model()` schema, without specifying whether this is a new field name or a replacement, and without updating the existing schema's processing rule.
findings.location: ## What — 'TWO LANES: prod_lane = MAX(correctness tier-tagged binaries) * likelihood (common=1.0/sometimes=0.6/rare=0.25)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a separate `prod_likelihood: str = Field(default='sometimes', description='common|sometimes|rare')` field to the new `code_review_verification_model()` schema, keeping the existing `likelihood` for backward compatibility with plan-review.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `impact_fn` is a **new** kernel seam to be added (not an existing one), specify exactly which function it is added to (`pass3_decide`, `pass3_over_findings`, or a new `code_review_decide` wrapper), and confirm backward-compatible defaulting so no existing caller breaks.
findings.finding: The `impact_fn` parameter on `pass3_decide()` and `pass3_over_findings()` does not exist in the current codebase; the plan describes it as 'the same `impact_fn` param added to `pass3_decide()`' — implying it already exists or is from a prior plan — but a search of the entire repo returns zero hits for `impact_fn` in source code. Both functions hard-call `impact(attrs)` internally with no injection seam.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics section: 'use the same `impact_fn` param added to `pass3_decide()`'; AC3: 'Per-gate dispatch via `impact_fn`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: '`impact_fn: Callable[[dict], float] = impact` is a new optional param on `pass3_over_findings` (default = existing `impact`, so all existing callers are byte-unchanged).' This makes the seam precise and acknowledges the addition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the new tier-tagged binary sub-answers can be added as optional fields (with `na` defaults) to the existing shared `verification_model` instead of creating a separate `code_review_verification_model`, following the established `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT` extension pattern used in ADR 0032.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `code_review_verification_model()` as a new structured-output contract 'extending the shared model', creating a second verification contract for a single gate. The kernel's own docstring explicitly enforces 'ONE binary vocabulary + ONE `verification` output contract that every review surface shares, eliminating the silent-drift class' — a second registered contract for code review directly undermines this stated invariant.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics: 'a `code_review_verification_model()` extending the shared model'; Scope: '`verify.py` (`code_review_verification_model`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `verification_model`'s `Binary` with the new tier-tagged fields (e.g. `data_loss_without_recovery`, `security_bypass`, etc.) using the `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT` frozenset so plan-review verifiers that don't set them abstain (excluded from validity mean). No second contract; no new registration; no drift surface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the provenance of `silent_failure` and `escapes_automation` explicitly: either label them DET (with a detection rule) or LLM-emitted (adding them to the `code_review_verification_model` binary schema alongside the tier-tagged sub-questions).
findings.finding: The `amp` formula depends on `silent_failure` and `escapes_automation` boolean signals, but their provenance is never specified. The plan labels reversibility/reach/change-frequency as 'DET/hybrid (diff + git)' but is silent on whether `silent_failure` and `escapes_automation` are LLM-emitted fields (requiring schema placement in `code_review_verification_model`) or deterministic signals (requiring a detection specification). Without this, `amp` is unimplementable without an assumption.
findings.location: What section — DETECTION AMPLIFIER definition; Implementation mechanics — 'SUB-ANSWER SCHEMA'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an end-to-end AC (or extend AC3) that runs `rebar review` on a real diff with `impact_fn=impact_code` active and asserts the structured output contains `code_review_verification_model` fields and a non-null `impact_code` value, exercising the live dispatch path including DET git-signal extraction.
findings.finding: The cutover that routes code-review findings through `impact_fn=impact_code` (the new path) has no acceptance criterion that exercises this dispatch end-to-end with real findings through the live model — AC4 only runs a simulation against an offline labeled fixture, which bypasses the new LLM-call path and the DET git-signal extraction.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs); ## Implementation mechanics — PER-GATE DISPATCH bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve how `code_review_verification_model` coexists with the existing `contracts.register_contract('verification', verification_model)` — specify whether it registers under a distinct key, how `verify_findings()` is directed to use it for code-review gates, and confirm this does not mutate the shared contract the plan-review gate depends on.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `code_review_verification_model()` 'extending the shared model' will emit tier-tagged binary fields inside the single aggregate Pass-2 structured output without any added LLM calls, but does not resolve whether the existing `contracts.register_contract` machinery supports gate-specific contract extensions alongside the shared `verification` contract — a capability assertion left unverified.
findings.location: ## Implementation mechanics — SUB-ANSWER SCHEMA; ## Scope — verify.py (code_review_verification_model)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact function signature change (which function receives `impact_fn`, its default value, and the call-chain threading) and confirm where the 'plan-review byte-unchanged' test assertion is pinned.
findings.finding: The `impact_fn` parameter said to be added to `pass3_decide()` for per-gate dispatch does not exist in the current `pass3_decide()` signature, and the plan does not specify where in `decide.py` this parameter is inserted or how the existing call sites (plan-review, code-review `code_review_decide` step) are migrated without breakage.
findings.location: ## Implementation mechanics — 'PER-GATE DISPATCH: use the same `impact_fn` param added to `pass3_decide()` (default = existing `impact`); the code-review wrapper passes `impact_fn=impact_code`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'create a test asserting plan-review impact function output is byte-for-byte unchanged before and after the `impact_fn` dispatch introduction' to the Scope section, since the test it references ('asserted by test') does not currently exist.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'plan-review byte-unchanged (asserted by test)' as a scope exclusion guarantee, but no such test exists anywhere in the test suite. A search for tests asserting plan-review impact byte-identity against the new `impact_fn` dispatch returns no matches.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics: 'plan-review byte-unchanged (asserted by test)'; AC3: 'plan-review byte-unchanged (asserted by test)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a documented fallback for `churn_90d` when the git-log call fails or returns no data (e.g., default to `churn_90d = 0` yielding `freq_mult = 0.5`, or `churn_90d = 15` for neutral mid-range), and add an AC or note covering the shallow-clone / non-git case.
findings.finding: The `churn_90d` git-log call has no specified fallback value when git is unavailable (shallow clone, non-git environment, permission failure), leaving the `freq_mult` formula undefined/error-prone on failure.
findings.location: ## What — CHANGE-FREQUENCY MULTIPLIER section; ## Scope — 'DET signals'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the CHANGE-FREQUENCY MULTIPLIER spec: 'If git is unavailable or the file has no history, `churn_90d` defaults to 0 (freq_mult = 0.5, the neutral floor).' Surface a warning log when the fallback fires.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `git log` flag form for the `churn_90d` count (e.g., `git log --format='' --after='90 days ago' -- <path>` vs `--follow`), state the environment precondition (full git history required), and name a fallback value for `freq_mult` (or `churn_90d`) when git is unavailable or the repo is shallow.
findings.finding: The `churn_90d` DET signal relies on a `git log` call that is unspecified at the flag level and has no stated fallback for environments where git history is incomplete or unavailable (shallow clone, detached worktree, permission failure), leaving the `freq_mult` formula undefined at runtime in common CI setups.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics section — 'churn_90d = number of commits touching the file in the last 90 days (from `git log`, DET)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation mechanics section: 'churn_90d = `git log --format='' --after="90 days ago" -- <path> | wc -l`; requires full history (not shallow clone); fallback: churn_90d=15 (mid-range → freq_mult=0.75) when git is unavailable or returns an error, logged at WARNING.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specified fallback for `churn_90d` when `git log` is unavailable or returns an error (e.g., default to `churn_90d=0` with a logged warning, or `freq_mult=1.0` to fail-safe toward stricter scoring), and document this in the plan.
findings.finding: The `freq_mult` DET signal (`churn_90d` from `git log`) has no specified fallback when git history is unavailable (shallow clone, no git repo, git error), unlike every other git-dependent path in the codebase which either raises LLMConfigError or explicitly falls back.
findings.location: ## What — 'CHANGE-FREQUENCY MULTIPLIER (explicit formula): `freq_mult = 0.5 + 0.5 * min(churn_90d, 30) / 30` where churn_90d = number of commits touching the file in the last 90 days (from `git log`, DET)'
findings.suggested_fix: Default `churn_90d=0` (yielding `freq_mult=0.5`) on any git error, log a WARNING, and note this in the formula definition so implementors don't choose ad-hoc values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the 'DET' label from the churn_90d description (it implies existing infrastructure) and add an explicit task in Scope to implement the `git log --since` count call plus a specified fallback value (e.g. `freq_mult=1.0` or `0.5`) when git history is unavailable.
findings.finding: The plan states `churn_90d = number of commits touching the file in the last 90 days (from `git log`, DET)`, implying a DET (deterministic/existing) infrastructure path for per-file git log commit counting. No such path exists in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/` — the only git utility used there is `run_git` for diff-reading in `assemble.py`. There is no existing DET mechanism for commit-frequency signals.
findings.location: What section: 'CHANGE-FREQUENCY MULTIPLIER: `freq_mult = 0.5 + 0.5 * min(churn_90d, 30) / 30` where churn_90d = number of commits touching the file in the last 90 days (from `git log`, DET)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Scope the reversibility_floor to apply only when the *finding itself* is about the irreversible surface (e.g., the finding's serious-binary is `unversioned-published-contract-break` or `data-loss-without-recovery`), not whenever the changed file happens to be in a migration dir — otherwise nits on migration files are structurally mis-scored at 0.6.
findings.finding: The `reversibility_floor = 0.6` fires unconditionally for any change touching a hard-to-reverse surface (migration dir, exported API, deletion, etc.), overriding the amp correction and the tier-based calibration. A nit-level finding (e.g. cosmetic rename in a migration file) produces: maint_lane = 0.3 * 0.5 = 0.15, amp-reduced = 0.15 * 0.8 = 0.12, then floor raises to 0.6 — directly contradicting the stated re-anchoring goal of 'a nit ~0.15-0.2'.
findings.location: What section — REVERSIBILITY FLOOR definition and EXPLICIT COMPOSE ORDER; also AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a guard: `reversibility_floor = 0.6 if (touches_hard_surface AND MAX(tier-tagged binaries) >= serious_tier) else 0`. Alternatively, define the floor as additive bonus (`impact = min(1.0, max(impact_base * amp, impact_base * amp + reversibility_bonus))`) capped so it only elevates findings that are already moderate or above.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a schema-level definition of all 10 new carved sub-questions (field names, allowed values, and explicit lane assignment: correctness vs maintainability) to the plan, co-located with the `code_review_verification_model()` spec.
findings.finding: The plan's carved sub-question vocabulary (10 new binary names: data-loss-without-recovery, security-bypass-not-enforced-elsewhere, etc.) introduces a new enum not defined in any existing schema, and the plan does not specify their per-field output type, allowed values, or how they map to prod_lane vs maint_lane within the schema definition.
findings.location: ## What — 'CARVED SUB-QUESTION SET (audit-approved; each serious binary carries its charitable carve-out): serious = {data-loss-without-recovery, ...}; moderate/minor = {capability-degraded, contract-drift, hidden-invariant, implicit-coupling, dead-code}'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `impact_fn` on `pass3_decide` serves >=3 call-sites or can instead be applied inside the `code_review_decide` workflow step (computing `impact_code` before passing verifications to `pass3_over_findings`) so the shared kernel is not modified for a single-gate concern.
findings.finding: The `impact_fn` callable injection on `pass3_decide()` is a premature abstraction with zero existing call-sites: `pass3_decide` currently has no such parameter (grep confirms), plan-review is explicitly kept byte-unchanged (using the default), and no third gate is named in this plan. Rule-of-Three requires >=3 call-sites before extracting a parameterized seam into a shared kernel function.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics section: 'use the same `impact_fn` param added to `pass3_decide()` (default = existing `impact`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Move `impact_code` computation into the `code_review_decide` workflow step: reshape verifications, apply `impact_code` to rewrite each `severity_attributes` into a synthetic scalar, then pass to the existing `pass3_over_findings` with `threshold_for` unchanged. The kernel gains no new parameter; plan-review is untouched by construction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the five outside-kernel callers of `pass3_decide`/`pass3_over_findings`/`impact` (`plan_review/orchestrator.py`, `plan_review/workflow_ops.py`, `plan_review/passes.py`, `workflow/gate_dispatch.py`, `evals/eval_scorers.py`) in Scope and confirm each is either unchanged or explicitly listed as an edit target.
findings.finding: The plan enumerates only `decide.py` and `verify.py` in Scope, but adding `impact_fn` to `pass3_decide` or `pass3_over_findings` — or changing how `impact()` is dispatched inside those functions — would require updates to at least five callers outside `review_kernel/`: `plan_review/orchestrator.py`, `plan_review/workflow_ops.py`, `plan_review/passes.py`, `workflow/gate_dispatch.py`, and `evals/eval_scorers.py`. None of these are listed in Scope.
findings.location: Scope section: 'src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py (`impact_code` via `impact_fn`), verify.py (`code_review_verification_model`), the code-review verify prompt, the DET signals, the labeled fixture + re-anchoring calibration, and the ADR. Plan-review impact unchanged.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Callers of `pass3_over_findings` / `impact` outside `review_kernel/` are unchanged — the new `impact_fn` param defaults to `impact`, so no call-site edit is required. Confirmed: orchestrator.py, workflow_ops.py, passes.py, gate_dispatch.py, eval_scorers.py.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or extend AC1 to explicitly assert that the 0.33 attribute floor is removed (e.g., a unit test asserting that a pure-nit finding with all binaries false scores below 0.25 without any floor intervention).
findings.finding: The 'What' section explicitly names 'drop the 0.33 attribute floor' as a deliverable of the re-anchoring work, but no AC criterion verifies its removal. An implementer could retain the old floor and pass all four ACs.
findings.location: What section (re-anchor bullet) vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC4)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1: 'The legacy 0.33 severity-attribute floor MUST NOT be present in impact_code; a unit test with all tier-tagged binaries false and amp=0.8 and no reversibility_floor surface MUST score ≤ 0.24 (0.0 * 0.8 = 0.0, clamped to 0.0).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Centralise all calibration constants (tier values, freq_mult saturation, reversibility_floor, amp, block threshold) into a single named-constant block or config object so recalibration requires a one-place change, not a grep-and-patch across files.
findings.finding: The tier values (0.3/0.6/0.9), freq_mult saturation point (30 commits), reversibility_floor (0.6), amp (0.8/1.0), and block threshold are hardcoded magic numbers in the implementation. The plan explicitly states the labeled set 'grows via the data-capture corpus' and mandates re-anchoring co-calibration, implying these values are expected to evolve — but no configurability mechanism (e.g., a constants module, config file, or single named-constant block) is specified, so each recalibration requires hunting down and patching scattered literals.
findings.location: ## What — TIER VALUES / CHANGE-FREQUENCY MULTIPLIER / REVERSIBILITY FLOOR / DETECTION AMPLIFIER; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `CODE_REVIEW_IMPACT_PARAMS` dataclass or `constants.py` section (e.g., `TIER_MINOR=0.3`, `TIER_MODERATE=0.6`, `TIER_SERIOUS=0.9`, `FREQ_MULT_SATURATION=30`, `REVERSIBILITY_FLOOR=0.6`, `AMP_DETECTABLE=0.8`, `BLOCK_THRESHOLD=<value>`) imported by both `impact_code` and the calibration script. Reference these names in the compose expression in AC1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the hedged AC4 comparison with a specific, measurable criterion: e.g. 'simulation script reports `mean(impact_code[HIGH]) − mean(impact_code[NIT]) ≥ 0.35` on the labeled fixture, with the raw scores logged to stdout for audit; remove the `or the gap is explained` escape clause.'
findings.finding: AC4 contains a hedge ('or the gap is explained') that allows the criterion to be satisfied without demonstrating measurable separation improvement, and 'better' lacks a specific metric (AUC, separation gap, correct-classification rate), making the criterion non-binary and effort-frameable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and specify exactly where `impact_fn` is introduced — the current `pass3_decide` signature has no such param; add a concrete before/after signature to the plan so the scope of caller changes is visible.
findings.finding: The plan says 'use the same `impact_fn` param added to `pass3_decide()`', implying `impact_fn` already exists as a parameter on that function. Reading the actual code shows `pass3_decide(verification, *, block_threshold, blocking_enabled)` — no `impact_fn` parameter exists. The dispatch mechanism must be designed and added, not merely 'used'.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics section: 'PER-GATE DISPATCH: use the same `impact_fn` param added to `pass3_decide()`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'use the same `impact_fn` param added to `pass3_decide()`' with the actual new signature, e.g. `pass3_over_findings(..., *, impact_fn=impact, threshold_for)` (at the findings-loop level, not the single-finding decide level), and enumerate which callers change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the fixture path to match the existing repo layout (`tests/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` rather than `tests/unit/fixtures/`), or explicitly state the directory is to be created and update the AC accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan names the fixture path `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl`, but the directory `tests/unit/fixtures/` does not exist (the repo has `tests/fixtures/` — a different path). No file named `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` exists anywhere in the repository.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics section: 'a curated fixture `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl`'; AC4: 'The `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` fixture exists'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the path to `tests/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` to match the existing `tests/fixtures/` directory, or add 'create `tests/unit/fixtures/`' as an explicit task in the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either correct the fixture path to an existing directory (e.g. `tests/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl`) or explicitly note that `tests/unit/fixtures/` must be created as a new directory.
findings.finding: The plan names `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` as a target artifact, but the directory `tests/unit/fixtures/` does not exist in the repository. The `tests/unit/` directory has no `fixtures/` subdirectory at all.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics section: 'a curated fixture `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl`'; AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out path for the code-review gate (e.g., describe that reverting impact_fn=impact_code to impact_fn=impact restores prior behavior, and document this as the rollback step in the ADR or a deployment note).
findings.finding: The code-review gate's switch to impact_code is a behavioral breaking change for that gate, but the plan provides no explicit rollback or back-out path for the code-review gate itself. The impact_fn=impact_code call will be hardcoded for code-review once deployed; reverting requires a re-deploy with no described procedure.
findings.location: Implementation Mechanics (per-gate dispatch paragraph) and Scope
findings.suggested_fix: In the ADR (or Implementation Mechanics), add: 'Rollback: set impact_fn=impact in the code-review wrapper and redeploy; this restores exact prior scoring for the code-review gate. The impact_fn default=impact in pass3_decide() ensures all other gates are unaffected.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for the 'no added LLM calls' claim, e.g. a unit/integration test that monkeypatches the LLM client, runs a code-review pass, and asserts the call count equals the pre-change baseline (e.g. `assert mock_llm.call_count == N`).
findings.finding: AC2 asserts 'no added LLM calls' but names no proving command or mechanism to verify call count, making this claim uncheckable without manual code inspection.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the fixture update lifecycle: who/what process appends entries (manual curation, automated pipeline, or PR-gated), whether writes are atomic (e.g., append-then-validate script), and whether there is a size/deduplication policy to prevent unbounded growth.
findings.finding: The `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` fixture's update mechanism ('grows via the data-capture corpus') has no defined ownership, triggering process, or concurrency safety, leaving it undefined who appends entries and how conflicts are resolved.
findings.location: ## Implementation mechanics + calibration data — CALIBRATION/LABELED SET; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'fixture ownership' note to the ADR or the calibration section: entries are appended only via a named script that validates JSONL integrity, new labels require human sign-off (PR review), and the fixture is capped at N entries with oldest-eviction or archive policy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Why not alternatives' subsection to the ADR (or plan) contrasting the two-lane MAX model against at least: (a) CVSS-style product-form aggregation, (b) weighted-mean on enriched domain attributes, and (c) domain-specific re-calibration of the existing `impact()` without structural redesign — stating why each was rejected.
findings.finding: The two-lane MAX (prod_lane/maint_lane) architecture is presented without comparing it to established alternative approaches — such as CVSS's Base Score aggregation (exploitability × impact subscores), a weighted-mean on enriched attributes, or domain-specific re-calibration of the existing `impact()` attributes — and without citing relevant prior art on multi-lane severity scoring. The 'Why not alternatives' argument the plan-review ADRs consistently provide (e.g. ADR 0010 §'Why not the alternatives') is absent.
findings.location: ## What — TWO LANES + TIER VALUES section; ## Why section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the ADR (or plan) a brief justification for the specific `freq_mult` parameters (0.5 floor, 30-commit saturation), citing either relevant prior art (hotspot analysis, DORA) or empirical sensitivity results from the labeled fixture — and note if these are intentionally tunable constants.
findings.finding: The `freq_mult` formula (`0.5 + 0.5 * min(churn_90d, 30) / 30`) encodes three specific numerical choices — the 0.5 floor, the 0.5 scaling weight, and the 30-commit saturation — with no citation to, or comparison with, established file-churn severity heuristics (e.g. hotspot analysis as documented by Tornhill, or DORA's change-failure-rate correlation studies). These values are asserted as design choices without evidence that they outperform simpler alternatives (e.g. a log scale, a binary hot/cold split, or a higher saturation point).
findings.location: ## What — CHANGE-FREQUENCY MULTIPLIER (explicit formula)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback behavior for `reversibility_floor` when DET surface detection errors or is unavailable (e.g., default to 0 with a warning, or fail-closed at 0.6), co-located with the DET signal spec.
findings.finding: The `reversibility_floor` DET surface detection (migration dir, exported public API, persisted/serialization format, deletion, released artifact) has no specified fallback when the DET step errors or is unavailable, unlike the existing security detector path which has explicit fail-closed/fail-open modes.
findings.location: ## What — 'REVERSIBILITY FLOOR (det/hybrid): if the change touches a hard-to-reverse surface (migration dir, exported public API, persisted/serialization format, deletion, released artifact), `reversibility_floor = 0.6`, else 0'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default co-calibration procedure (e.g. 'sweep block_threshold ∈ [0.5, 0.9] in 0.05 steps, pick the value that maximises separation gap = mean_HIGH_score − mean_NIT_score on `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl`') so the executor has a deterministic, reproducible algorithm.
findings.finding: The co-calibration process for tier values and the block threshold is unspecified — 'co-calibrate tier values + block threshold on the labeled set' names the goal but gives no algorithm (manual tuning? grid search? threshold optimization by F1/AUC?). An executor must choose an approach, which is a placeholder decision.
findings.location: ## What — RE-ANCHOR bullet; ## Implementation mechanics — CALIBRATION/LABELED SET bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the CALIBRATION bullet: 'Co-calibration procedure: fix tier values at the stated defaults (0.3/0.6/0.9), then sweep `block_threshold` from 0.5 to 0.9 in 0.05 steps; select the value that maximises `mean(impact_code[HIGH]) − mean(impact_code[NIT])` on the labeled fixture. Record the chosen threshold in the ADR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for AC1, e.g. `pytest tests/unit/test_impact_code.py -v` referencing a parametrized fixture that exercises at minimum: maint_lane wins, prod_lane wins, amp=0.8 path, reversibility_floor binding, and saturation at 1.0.
findings.finding: AC1 states the exact compose formula but provides no concrete proving command — no test invocation or script is described that would produce evidence the formula is implemented correctly (e.g. a unit test with fixed inputs and expected outputs).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
